coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots functionalized tapered optical fiber for highly sensitive and specific detection of Leptospira DNA
coredata.dc:creator: Zainuddin, Nurul Hida
Chee, Hui Yee
Rashid, Suraya Abdul
Ahmad, Muhammad Zamharir
Bakar, Muhammad Hafiz Abu
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
Yaacob, Mohd Hanif
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report on the development of DNA biosensor using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance Leptospira DNA detection. Leptospira is a bacterium that invades human body and cause leptospirosis disease. Leptospirosis has attracted attention mainly due to several cases of human mortality which have distressed health professionals. This biosensor system employs tapered optical fiber as a sensing transducer. The CQDs were functionalized at the tapered region via in situ deposition (drop-casted) before incubation using commercialized chemicals to link the 16 s ribosomal RNA gene (rrs) probe DNA. Detection was achieved with the occurrence of hybridization between the probe DNA and different concentrations of complementary DNA (Leptospira DNA). This biosensor with limit of detection (LOD) of 1.0 fM demonstrated better sensitivity at 1.8295 nm/nM and higher affinity as its dissociation constant (Kd) of 0.552 × 10−09 M is lower compared to previous reported biosensors without CQDs. High specificity biosensor is validated when minimal shift was observed for non-complementary DNA. This novel biosensor was also able to distinguish between genomic DNA of Leptospira serovars and Clostridium difficile as control sample. The proposed biosensor has successfully exhibited the prominent role of nanomaterial for sensor enhancement. Consequently, the biosensor approach in this study has emphasized the potential for the development of more sensitive and reliable leptospirosis diagnostic testing in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tapered optical fiber
Optical biosensor
Carbon quantum dots
Leptospirosis
Leptospira DNA

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber
Surface modification of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber
Optical characterization of tapered optical fiber
Characterization of tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs
Optical characterization of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Diagnostic Molecular Lab, Biotechnology and Nanotechnology Centre

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Ministry of Higher Education [PRGS/2/2019/STG05/UPM/03/1]. We like to acknowledge Diagnostic Molecular Lab, Biotechnology and Nanotechnology Centre, MARDI, Serdang for accommodate us to use the facilities for sample preparation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of single mode optical fiber tapered profile.
Optical biosensor system.
Modification of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber prior to probe DNA binding.
Transmission spectra for untapered and tapered SMF profile 5–15-5 mm with waist diameter of 12 μm.
The surface morphologies of (A) CQDs, (B) magnified CQDs and (C) thickness of CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber.
2D and 3D AFM images of non-CQDs functionalized (A) and (B) and CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber (C) and (D).
EDS spectrum of (A) non-CQDs functionalized and (B) tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs.
Raman spectrum obtained when Raman spectroscopy analysis was performed on tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs.
Schematic diagram of light propagation in CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber.
Shift of interference spectra of tapered optical fiber after CQDs coating.
Transmission spectra of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber immersed in NaCl.
Relationship between wavelength shifts against refractive index of NaCl for tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs.
Transmission spectra of probe DNA/cDNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber.
Micro-characterization of hybridized cDNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber (A) FESEM, (B) AFM, and (C) EDS.
Raman spectra of cDNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber.
Relationship between wavelength shift and cDNA concentrations.
Binding affinity of cDNA using (A) non-CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber and (B) CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber.
Histogram of wide dynamic range of hybridized cDNA corresponds to its wavelength shifts.
Histogram of the sensor specificity.
Histogram of the sensor cross-reactivity.
Oligonucleotide sequences of Leptospira DNA.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots functionalized tapered optical fiber for highly sensitive and specific detection of Leptospira DNA

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Biotechnology and Nanotechnology Research Center, Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute (MARDI), 43400 Serdang, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report on the development of DNA biosensor using carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance Leptospira DNA detection. Leptospira is a bacterium that invades human body and cause leptospirosis disease. Leptospirosis has attracted attention mainly due to several cases of human mortality which have distressed health professionals. This biosensor system employs tapered optical fiber as a sensing transducer. The CQDs were functionalized at the tapered region via in situ deposition (drop-casted) before incubation using commercialized chemicals to link the 16 s ribosomal RNA gene (rrs) probe DNA. Detection was achieved with the occurrence of hybridization between the probe DNA and different concentrations of complementary DNA (Leptospira DNA). This biosensor with limit of detection (LOD) of 1.0 fM demonstrated better sensitivity at 1.8295 nm/nM and higher affinity as its dissociation constant (Kd) of 0.552 × 10−09 M is lower compared to previous reported biosensors without CQDs. High specificity biosensor is validated when minimal shift was observed for non-complementary DNA. This novel biosensor was also able to distinguish between genomic DNA of Leptospira serovars and Clostridium difficile as control sample. The proposed biosensor has successfully exhibited the prominent role of nanomaterial for sensor enhancement. Consequently, the biosensor approach in this study has emphasized the potential for the development of more sensitive and reliable leptospirosis diagnostic testing in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Leptospirosis is an infectious disease caused by pathogenic bacteria of the genus Leptospira [1]. Frequent outbreaks have occurred worldwide over the last decade. A study from F. Costa et al. concluded that leptospirosis causes 1.03 million cases and 58,900 deaths each year globally [2]. Specifically in Malaysia, recent statistic from the Ministry of Health (MOH) shows that in 2011, there were 2,268 cases of infection that had dramatically increased to 8,291 cases in 2015 with 55 deaths recorded [3]. These statistical data is alarming as this infectious disease has exhibited an increasing trend over the years. It has emerged as the most prevalent zoonotic disease that becomes a major cause of human morbidity and mortality, hence leading to serious public health concern. A factor that upsurges the perceived threat of the leptospirosis is the struggle in differentiating their clinical signs. Leptospirosis is often disregarded in diagnosis as its early signs and symptoms mimic better-known viral illnesses such as influenza, dengue fever, and malaria. These puzzling issues lead to misdiagnosis and hence, delay the treatment. Therefore, to expedite the appropriate treatment, sensitive and specific detection is at the utmost necessity in medical diagnostic for leptospirosis.

The current diagnostic available is inadequate to get rapid and accurate detection. For example, routine blood tests are usually nonspecific and while bacterial culture and microagglutination test (MAT) are specific, they are impractical to be implemented for early diagnostic tests. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) based test, on the other hand, has established its capability for rapid and accurate diagnosis. Nevertheless, PCR-based test is not yet widely available and expensive. Therefore, an alternative and more reliable scientifically based test for leptospirosis is highly desirable. Biosensor could be an option to achieve that requirement. Biosensor system such as conductometric, potentiometric, and amperometry are the common electrical-based transducing platforms [4]. This conventional system is low in cost and highly sensitive but it is prone to electromagnetic interference as well as danger during conducting test. Several applications of optical biosensor in the biotechnology and molecular biology realm seem promising to identify sensitive and specific detection. The inherent advantages of optical approach as compared to its electrical counterparts are explosion proof, immunity to radiofrequency disturbance and electromagnetic interference, as well as also their environmental ruggedness and resistance. One of the most effective optical biosensors is by using optical fiber as the transducing platform. Its abilities such as in situ operation, remote sensing application, distributed sensing potential, reduced setup dimension (small size, light weight, and flexible), minimal invasion for in vivo measurement while providing high sensitivity and specificity, have created strong interest towards biosensors based on optical fiber.

However, optical fiber requires some physical modifications to function as a sensor. The preferable method is tapering using fusion splicer technique, which is simpler and more controlled modification on optical fiber than any other methods such as grating and etching. The steps of heating and pulling are highly controllable with the use of glass processing workstation. Moreover, the light propagation interruption in the core and evanescence wave enhancement on the fiber surrounding are achieved at the same time. On the contrary, gratings method interrupts the light propagation in the core and to enhance the evanescent wave, it will require extra processes such as bending, polishing and chemical etching. Meanwhile, etching method requires tedious and risky preparation as the chemicals used are highly corrosive and can cause severe burns if splashed on the skin or in the eyes.

Further modification on tapered optical fiber is by coating with nanomaterials leading to enhanced biosensors with sensitivity surpassing traditional sensor functionalization [5,6]. Thus, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are introduced as a new functionalization material. This new carbon nanostructure has attracted growing interest and led to more research on this material. CQDs as a new class nanomaterial could provide fresh perspective in the development of effective leptospirosis detection. They are quasi-spherical nanoparticles, formed by crystalline sp2 graphite cores, or sp3 carbon. The sp2 and sp3 indicate the number of s and p orbitals mixed to create new, degenerate hybrid orbitals. These allotropes come with surface functional groups, in particular with oxygen-containing molecules [7] such as carboxylates (–COOH) or hydroxyl (–OH) derivatives. This then grants them with feasibility in covalent bonding with further surface functionalization. The honeycomb architecture of the CQDs also allows more efficient adsorption of molecules. Moreover, unique physical characteristic of their nanosized particle in nanoscale range (about 10 nm) has attracted great attention towards them. This superior feature assists in increasing surface area of the sensing region. Thus, it is likely to have higher surface to volume ratio as compared to amorphous films. As a result of the interaction between the CQDs-nanostructured layers on the sensor platform with the analyte, the sensor output could be enhanced significantly. By virtue, higher sensitivity is normally expected for this nanotechnology approach. Other than that, in credits to their benign, abundant and inexpensive nature [8], it basically has safe, unlimited source with low production cost for application in biosensing. Thus, deployments of unique tapered optical fiber as a transducer, together with CQDs nanomaterial gives an advantage toward producing novel and high performance biosensor.

In this work, standard single-mode optical fiber (SMF) was employed. The standard SMF has a core diameter of ∼ 9 µm and a cladding diameter of 125 µm. It was modified by tapering its body using optical fiber glass processor workstation (Vytran GPX-3400). The machine altered the optical fiber dimension by pulling the two ends of the optical fiber while it was heated by a special filament. The real-time control system allowed a customized dimension, uniformity and reproducibility of the fabricated taper by commanding the pulling speed and heat at a constant value of 1 mm/s and 42 W, respectively. The tapered section was fabricated at the center of 1 m of optical fiber with symmetric up and down taper transition of 5 mm length as presented in Fig. 1 . These dimensions have been optimized for ligand-target protein interaction as reported in previous work [9].

The fabricated tapered optical fiber region was then positioned within the straight micro-channel groove of customized sample holder. Both ends of the tapered optical fiber were connected to the single mode FC/PC patch cords using the optical fiber fusion splicer machine (Sumitomo Electric Industries ltd.). The input end of the patch cord was fixed to the broadband light source (Amonics ALS 18-B-FA) with wavelength ranged from 1500 − 1600 nm. Meanwhile the output end was fixed to the optical spectrum analyser (OSA) (Yokogawa AQ6331). The OSA displays the transmission response and recorded by a customised LabViewTM program in the computer. The set-up system is shown in Fig. 2 . After the system was completely set up, the optical properties of standard SMF and tapered optical fiber were investigated by recording their transmission response.

Next, the fabricated tapered optical fiber was deposited with CQDs. The CQDs was provided in distilled water solution obtained from Institute of Advanced Technology (ITMA), UPM with concentration of 1000 ppm. Drop casted method facilitated with optical deposition techniques were employed to coat tapered optical fiber with CQDs. In the same set up system (Fig. 2), micro-channel groove was temporarily replaced by glass slide for this deposition purpose. After the tapered optical fiber region was secured on the glass slide, 10 μL CQDs solution was drop casted. Concurrently, light was transmitted through the tapered optical fiber from the broadband light source. This would allow the interaction of intensified evanescent waves and CQDs which grounds optical trapping, and good convection flow to trap the CQDs onto the tapered optical fiber surface [10,11]. The fiber was left to dry at 24 °C room temperature with the light source kept on throughout the period. It took about 5 min to fully dry the deposited CQDs. After that, the tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs was positioned back into the micro-channel groove to be rinsed with deionized water for 3 times and dried again at room temperature. These steps were also conducted without turning off the light source. The rinsing step is substantial to remove any non-adsorbed excess CQDs on the sensing region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: The micro-characterization of tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs was investigated using Hitachi Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM). The testing was done at MIMOS Berhad. The Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was used to characterise and quantify the surface characteristic of the sample such as roughness. Roughness is frequently defined as closely spaced irregularities or with terms such as “irregular”, “uneven”, “coarse in texture”, “broken by prominences”, and other equally ones [12]. Its value was measured through AFM measurements. The characterization was done using AFM machine at Quasi-S Sdn. Bhd., UKM MTDC. Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) technique was used to identify the elemental composition of CQDs. In this work, Horiba EX-250X-MAX80 located at MIMOS Berhad was employed to verify the existing element in such materials. The EDS system is incorporated with the Hitachi FESEM equipment. The optical properties of tapered optical fiber were also investigated before and after CQDs deposition. After tapered optical fiber was functionalized with CQDs, it was then optically characterized by comparing its sensitivity using sodium chloride (NaCl) solution with different refractive index (RI). The CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber was immersed in several NaCl (Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, MO, USA) concentrations (0.2, 0.4, 0.6 and 0.8 M). The sensitivity was achieved when the transmission output of every NaCl concentration was being compared with its respective RI. Prior to characterization testing, the RI of each NaCl concentration was measured by using handheld optical refractrometer (ATAGO B028977, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface modification of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Surface modification process was constituted to promote the conjugation between inorganic (CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber) and organic molecules (Leptospira DNA). This was the initial stage in biosensor establishment before introducing Leptospira DNA onto CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. The modification of the CQDs surface was performed through covalent bonding principle to bind with probe DNA as reported in our previous work [13]. Briefly, as illustrated in Fig. 3 , the principle of CQDs surface binding to probe DNA is using several bi-functional linkers. The first linker is sodium chloride (NaOH) to enrich the layer of the active hydroxyl group (–OH) on the CQDs. Then, followed by silanizing using (3-Aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) to form a silane monolayer so that it can be treated with glutaraldehyde (GA). After the GA treatment, the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is finally readied for probe DNA attachment.

Next, the modified CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber was immobilized with probe DNA prior to target DNA hybridization. The probe DNA and target DNA are the essences in mechanism of biosensor detection. The probe DNA captures and binds to specific sequence that matches with their respective sequence. The target DNA strand creates a tethered nucleotide with the single-stranded probe DNA via hybridization process, forming a new duplex molecule, which then can be detected. The probe DNA used in this work was particularly designed and aligned based on 16S ribosomal RNA (rrs) gene as referred to previous literature [14]. The targets DNA consisted of complementary DNA (cDNA) and their non-complementary DNA (non-cDNA) as well as real genomic Leptospira bacteria DNA sample. The oligonucleotide sequences of Leptospira DNA used in this work were also similar with our previous work [13] in order to allow comparison of biosensor performance. All the probe DNA sequence with their respective designed sequence cDNA of rrs gene and its non-cDNA sequences were tabulated in Table 1 . The synthetic DNA was fabricated by Integrated DNA technologies, purchased via Apical Scientific Sdn Bhd.

The cDNA and non-cDNA were used to test binding occurrence with probe DNA before being tested with the genomic bacteria DNA. 1 μM probe DNA was immobilized onto modified CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber by incubating the probe DNA for 1 h. After that, the tapered region was rinsed three times using deionized water and left to dry at room temperature. Following that, several concentrations of target cDNA (1 μM to 0.1 fM) and 1.0 nM non-cDNA were incubated for 1 h. Again, the tapered region was rinsed three times with deionized water after incubated in each concentration of target DNA and then dried at room temperature. For each concentration, the testing was performed with a new set of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. All experiments were replicated three times and conducted at room temperature monitored by a digital thermometer.

Following that, genomic DNA from different bacteria was used to ensure that the biosensor did not detect other than the Leptospira target site as this could lead to sensor unfeasibility. Two types of genomic DNA tested were from pathogenic Leptospira interrogans serovar; Copenhageni and Canicola. For negative control sample, Clostridium difficile (C. difficile) DNA; bacteria that infects the human intestine was employed. The entire genomic DNA was acquired from Virology Laboratory, Universiti Putra Malaysia. The genomic DNA had denatured double-stranded DNA by thermally soaking them at 95 °C water for 2 min and rapidly cooled in ice water prior utilization to the detection system. After each immobilization and hybridization process was completed, the transmission response was recorded and the output spectrum was evaluated by quantifying the wavelength shift. Prior to optical detection, micro-characterizations of Leptospira DNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber was performed using FESEM, AFM, and EDS measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Optical response of standard SMF and tapered optical fiber is shown in Fig. 4 . Red line shows typical spectrum of tapered optical fiber with dimension of 5–15-5 mm (15 mm taper length and 5 mm down/up tapered regions) having waist diameter of 12 µm as mentioned in Section 2.1. Blue line is spectrum of untapered optical fiber (standard SMF) which also serves as a reference. The difference between both spectra clearly indicates that tapered optical fiber spectrum displays a periodic interferometry pattern which is absent in the spectrum of the untapered optical fiber. Thus, relative transmission spectrum of the tapered optical fiber with respect to untapered optical fiber is illustrated in Fig. 1 (green line). The optical response of the tapered optical fiber yields the transmission spectrum that is useful in quantifying Leptospira DNA detection.

The periodic behaviour of the optical response in Fig. 3 is governed by the similar principle of Mach-Zehnder interferometer that simulates the light propagation within a tapered optical fiber. As light reaches the down-taper region of the tapered fiber, due to the core size and v-number reduction, higher-order modes are excited at the tapered region. The combination of modes increases the evanescent field portion which then would react to the external surrounding. Upon reaching the up-taper region, thee modes are coupled together, causing the construction and destruction of waves resulting in a fringe-like output spectrum [5]. Thus, transmission, in this circumstance, is expressed as [15]:
(1)
I =
I 1
+
I 2
+ 2
I 1
I 2
c o s
( Δ Φ )
where I is the intensity of coupling modes of the fundamental and high-order modes intensity, I 1 and I 2 respectively and ΔΦ is the phase shift between the two modes. This is explained as:
(2)
Δ Φ =
2 π
Δ
n
eff
L w
λ
where Δn eff is the difference between the effective refractive index of the core and the external surrounding (
n
c
eff
-
n
ext
eff
)
and L w is the waist length of the tapered region. λ is the input wavelength of the light.

Thus, according to the Equation (2), if there is an increment of cladding refractive index of tapered region, it will affect Δneff and subsequently ΔΦ which then results in the red shift phenomenon. Hence, the differences in the wavelength shift can be utilized for evaluating sensing performance of the biosensor when a functionalized optical fiber is immobilized and hybridized with probe DNA and target DNA, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: FESEM measurement was conducted to investigate the surface morphology of the deposition outcome. Fig. 5 shows FESEM images of CQDs coated on the tapered optical fiber. In Fig. 5(A), the nanostructured CQDs are homogeneously dispersed on tapered optical fiber surface. The magnified image in Fig. 5(B) shows amorphous spheres of quantum dots with varying grain sizes from 9.4 nm to 10.7 nm. The cross-sectional image of the optical fiber tip coated with CQDs is displayed in Fig. 5(C) with thickness average of 51.23 nm. The findings indicate that the CQDs are well-adhered on top of the tapered optical fiber surface.

The topographies of tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs were investigated using AFM spectroscopy. Fig. 6 displays 2D and 3D images of non-CQDs functionalized optical fiber and CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. The CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is clearly distinguished with the presence of quasi-spherical shape in disperse distribution. RMS roughness value obtained for 2D topographies of non-CQDs functionalized and CQDs functionalized tapered region is 2.153 nm and 7.195 nm, respectively. While RMS roughness value for 3D topographies are 7.375 nm and 39.538 nm for non-CQDs functionalized and CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber, respectively. The RMS roughness value is increased after deposition of CQDs onto tapered region, revealing that the AFM measurement has successfully confirmed the presence of functionalized layer on the tapered region. The increasing surface roughness when nanomaterial is deposited can be associated with higher surface area attainment [16]. High surface area is significant in sensing performance to create more available surface active sites for further interaction. In this work, it could contribute to more DNA adsorption and subsequently, stronger sensing performance.

Fig. 7 represents the EDS results of the non-CQDs functionalized and CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. The changes of elements percentage of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and silicon (Si) are compared. The strong peak of C and high weight percentage (wt%) in CQDs indicates high amount of C content. The increase of C as well as decremente of O and Si wt% confirms that the thin film coated on the optical fiber is mainly consisted of CQDs.

Fig. 8 shows the Raman spectra of the CQDs coated on the tapered optical fiber. Peaks observed at 1350 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 are the designation D and G bands of CQDs, respectively. D band is usually associated with disorder carbon structure while G band is attributed to in-plane stretching vibration mode of crystalline graphite carbon [17]. The strong peak of G band in Raman spectrum postulates that the majority of CQDs produced in this manner are predominantly graphitic in nature [18]. The high density of carbon presence in graphite-like films and consequential in high mechanical strength [19], can firmly coat the tapered optical fiber surrounding. In turn, DNA could secure their adsorption onto the CQDs surface to produce high sensing performance. Collectively, as the crystalline structure of CQDs provides more stability [20] and also high surface area, more probe DNA immobilisation could occur to offer high DNA hybridization rate and ultimately increase the sensing sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Next, the RI sensitivity of the tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs was investigated using NaCl solution of different concentrations ranging from 0.1 to 0.5 M. Fig. 11 displays the transmission spectra when NaCl solutions were introduced to CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. The responses record red shift transmission output. Distinctive interval of wavelength shift is also observed with the average shift of 1.635 nm for each increasing concentration.

The phase matching condition upon the change in NaCl concentrations affects the change of RIs, thus altering Δneff in Equation 2.1. Fig. 12 illustrates the proportional wavelength shift (Δλ) of transmission spectra towards RI of NaCl for tapered optical fiber functionalized with CQDs. The sensitivity achieved is 2235.4 nm/RIU with linear response (R2) of more than 99 %. It is found that the use of CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber managed to enhance the sensitivity by approximately 20 % compared to non-CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber reported in several previous works [13 23]. This is due to the higher average wavelength shift when using CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber than non-CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber.

Fig. 13 is a spectra comparison of optical response taken after CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber encountered chemical modification, probe DNA immobilization and cDNA hybridization. As mentioned in Section 2.4, all the steps to chemically modify the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber was necessary to bind it with the probe DNA. For each cDNA concentration tested, multiple sets of the same experiment were conducted using different CQDs functionalized tapered optical fibers that have undergone the exact same surface modification process. The optical responses reveal the red shift of transmission spectra. The peak spectrum of immobilized probe DNA shifts as much 1.695 nm to the right from adjacent peak spectrum of CQDs functionalized. Further peak spectrum shift of hybridized cDNA is also observed to shift as much as 2.624 nm to the right. The wavelength shifts in those spectra have now confirmed the amendment of the tapered optical fiber surface through successful binding of targeted cDNA.

Such red shift occurrence verifies that light propagation of the tapered optical fiber is being varied. It is due to the change in effective RI of the tapered optical fiber by successful binding of targeted cDNA. Abundant of hybridized cDNA also change the localised photon energy. The additional layer formed by hybridized cDNA at the tapered region increases the energy absorption but induces the reduction in the photon energy. As a result, the light frequency is reduced and the transmission spectrum moves to longer wavelengths [21]. This might explain the red shift phenomenon.

Fig. 14 shows the results of micro-characterizations of cDNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber using FESEM, AFM and EDS. FESEM reveals the images of rough surface with appearance of elongated networks structure on the tapered optical fiber surface as shown in Fig. 14(A). Meanwhile, Fig. 14(B) shows the topography of hybridized DNA on the tapered fiber through the AFM imaging. 2D and 3D topographies show some degeneracy in term of CQDs grain sizes image resolution when compared to AFM topography prior DNA hybridization as prevail before in Fig. 6. This is due to the formation of additional layer on top of CQDs layer as cDNA is being hybridized. Both of the topographies exhibit rough surfaces with the 2D and 3D surface roughness from RMS values are 15.241 nm and 70.503 nm, respectively. Fig. 14(C) shows the EDS image with the elements composition of cDNA hybridization product on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber. It is observed that C element is still the most abundant element after cDNA hybridization on CQDs. The weight percentage of C after cDNA hybridization increases (56.74 %) from C of CQDs coated. This occurrence shows that the additional layer is moulded due to DNA attachment. The lower percentage of O element after cDNA is introduced onto CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber suggests the occurrence of hybridizations. The weight percentage of silicon (Si) element is also observed to be decreased to 15.84 %. Conclusively, the increasing C and decreasing O and Si prove that both cDNA hybridizations on CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber, form a layer of hybridized DNA. These findings are concurrent with Mustapha Kamil et. al, which also noted an increase of C weight percentage and reduction of O and Si weight percentage after the attachment of dengue virus Eprotein on tapered fiber [9].

The Raman spectrum of hybridized DNA on the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is shown in Fig. 15 . From the graph, two bands of CQDs; D and G bands still exist at 1357 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1. However, as compared to Raman spectrum of CQDs, numerous conformation vibrational modes of DNA bases and phosphate groups have been identified throughout 600 to 1700 cm−1. This advocates the addition of more DNA molecules onto the surface. Raman bands at 618 cm−1 and 931 cm−1 peaks are attributed to cytosine and guanine, respectively [24]. The phosphate backbone (PO4−) band appears at 1066 cm−1. A band at 1203 cm−1 is assigned to in-plane ring of cytosine[25]. Peak at 1288 cm−1 band appears due to the CN stretching vibration from thymine DNA base [26]. Peak at 1376 cm−1 band corresponds to thymine [27]. Band at 1465 cm−1 is formed from stretching of CN bond in thymine [28]. The Raman band observed at 1616 cm−1 is due to CO stretching of guanine [29].

The optical performance of hybridized DNA was assessed by evaluating the biosensor sensitivity. The sensitivity of this biosensor was determined based on its response towards different concentrations of cDNA (0.1 nM to 1.0 nM). This range of concentration was chosen as it indicates the effective sensing region by higher magnitude of wavelength shift when a wide dynamic range of concentration (0.1 fM to 1 µM) was used as found in our previous work [13]. Similar range was also chosen for sensitivity comparison. For this investigaton, the wavelength shift corresponding to each concentration was measured and plotted to fit a linear trend line. This set of experiment was conducted in triplicates set. The biosensor sensitivity with CQDs functionalized on the tapered region is displayed in Fig. 16 . The biosensor sensitivity exhibits excellent linearity of 0.986 and sensitivity value of 1.8295 ± 0.6 nm/nM. This biosensor configuration shows higher sensitivity than N. H. Zainuddin et al. (1.2876 nm/nM) that used non-CQDs functionalized tapered fiber [13].

A wide dynamic range set of data was also fitted to Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation in order to assess the affinity of the biosensor towards Leptospira cDNA. Binding affinity is a key in determining the efficiency of the biosensor and also denoting the biosensor sensitivity by calculating its dissociation constant (Kd). The Kd value is used to evaluate and rank order strength of bio-molecular interaction. The smaller the Kd value, the greater the binding ligands are attracted to and bind to each other. Fig. 17 shows Langmuir isotherm adsorption equation fitting for non-CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber (A) and CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber (B). The value of Kd achieved is 0.552 × 10−9 M for CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber which is lower than the value attained by non-CQDs functionalized sensor(0.909 × 10−9M). This concludes that CQDs is successful in enhancing the affinity of the sensor towards Leptospira cDNA.

The limit of detection (LOD) was identified by testing the wavelength shift of cDNA with a wide dynamic range concentration of Leptospira DNA. Fig. 18 displays the bar graph consisting of cDNA wavelength shift from 0 M to 1 µM concentrations on tapered optical fiber functionalized with the CQDs. Based on Fig. 18, it can be seen that there was only a slight difference in wavelength shifts at lower concentration (0 M – 0.1 nM) as compared to higher concentration (0.1 nM – 1.0 μM). This was expected as increasing the concentration of cDNA generally increases the DNA hybridization as there were more cDNA strands present to form the duplex DNA formation. This situation gives adequate detection and clear-cut wavelength shift. However, at lower concentration of cDNA, there were lesser cDNA strands to hybridize with the probe DNA. At some point, the decreased DNA hybridization led to indistinct wavelength shift [30]. LOD for this biosensor is determined at 1.0 fM concentration as the 0.1 fM wavelength shift is almost similar with 0 M (control concentration). Thus, the distinctive wavelength shift can only be seen from 1.0 fM onwards. Fluctuation range of the sensor is represented by the error bar of each concentration, which indicates the standard deviation derived from the experiment triplicate at similar room temperature of 24 °C (Fig. 18). At the declared LOD of 1.0 fM, wavelength shift of 0.588 nm with standard deviation of ± 0.08 nm was acquired. In comparison, the next higher exponential concentration had a wavelength shift of 0.696 nm with standard deviation of ± 0.07 nm. Therefore, it can be safely stated that the fluctuation range of the sensor did not exceed the wavelength shift corresponding to the LOD. A sensor is considered promising if it can detect cDNA at the lowest concentration possible and still capable to form DNA duplexes. The LOD of 1.0 fM is in fact the highest LOD achieved among other reported DNA biosensors that use nanomaterial coating such as electrical based sensor by Kurkina et. al, (2 fM) [31] and S. Guo et al. (3 nM) [32], as well as in optical biosensor by Y. Huang et al. (1 pM) [33] and L. Angela et al. (7.5 pM) [34].

Ultimately, CQDs has successfully enhanced the DNA detection with the attainment of higher sensitivity, higher affinity and low LOD. It is presumed that these findings are due to the larger surface area and more active functional sites contributed by the functionalization of CQDs sensing layer onto the sensing region. This promoted more binding site for probe DNA adsorption on the CQDs. As a result, DNA hybridization activity was increased and boosted the rate of DNA adsorption onto the fiber. With this phenomenon, increasing wavelength shift was observed and the multivalent approach of the binding affinity was enhanced [35].

Specificity is perhaps the most crucial characteristic of a biosensor. It is the ability of a bioreceptor to detect its specific analyte. As shown in Fig. 19 , the wavelength shifts of cDNA, non-cDNA, and sterile ultrapure water after binding with the immobilized probe on tapered optical fiber sensor coated with CQDs are presented. Same concentration of 1.0 nM was used for cDNA and non-cDNA to ensure fair comparison between them. Sterile ultrapure water, which is the buffer of DNA stands for 0 M concentration to act as reference.

From the graph, consistent slight shifts are clearly observed when the sensors are tested with non-cDNA and sterile ultrapure water on all sensor combinations. This information verifies the absence of DNA on the sensing surface. However, the wavelength shift of cDNA is much stronger as it produces an ambiguous response that indicates high specificity of the proposed sensor. This unspecific event is due to the hydrogen bonding between non-cDNA bases and target bases that could not be simultaneously bound because they are not complementary in structure.

In order to evaluate the cross-reactivity of the Leptospira DNA biosensor integrated CQDs, hybridization tests were performed with extracted genomic DNA. The sample of Leptospira interrogans serovar; Copenhageni and Canicola genomic DNA were used to further investigate the practicability of this system with real sample. The complementary regions of Leptospira genomic DNA were aligned and bonded with the probe DNA. Genomic DNA of C. difficile, a bacterium that infects the human intestine was manipulated as a non-target control. Fig. 20 displays the wavelength shifts obtained that corresponds to the genomic DNA samples at concentration of 6.5 pg/μL for each sample.

The graphs show that the Leptospira genomic DNA either Canicola or Copenhgaeni produces a significant wavelength shift when compared to the C. Difficile DNA on sensor configuration. DNA sample with concentration of 6.5 pg/ul is sufficient to distinguish the huge range response. This study has higher limit of detection (LOD) than previous study by X. Wang et al. that was only able to produce significant response at 1.7 ng/μL [36].

The wavelength shift of genomic DNA seems much smaller than wavelength shift of synthetic DNA as 1 nM is equivalent to 6.5 pg/µL. The issue is actually related to the long DNA sequence of genomic DNA itself, which leads to greater steric hindrance and lower adsorption of the target DNA [37]. The longer sequences of the DNA (more than 30 bases) also affect the efficiency of hybridization due to the coiling up of target molecule upon their binding on the sensing transducer [38]. The binding efficiency owing to steric hindrance at the surface and gives crowding effect on the sensing region. The agglutination of the genomic DNA resulted in fewer targets DNA reaching and binding with the probe DNA.

Nevertheless, this work has highlighted the feasibility of interfacial engineering with nanomaterial integration to obtain improved target recognition efficiency and upgraded detection sensitivity. It is clearly observed that by integrating CQDs on the sensing region, the wavelength shift of genomic Leptospira DNA hybridization was increased. As reported in our previous work, the wavelength of hybridized Leptospira DNA of Canicola and Copenhageni serovar is shifted by 1.175 nm and 1.055 nm, respectively, when non-CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is used [13]. In this work, the wavelength shift was increased by approximately 25 % to 1.587 nm for Canicola and 1.558 nm for Conpenhageni when CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is used. Therefore, the results obtained substantially prove the improvement of sensor's adaptability to detect longer genomic DNA sequence in real samples. From that, it can be concluded that the proposed tapered optical sensor coated with CQDs is able to successfully detect and distinguish Leptospira DNA from other bacteria species.

By introducing CQDs onto tapered optical fiber, strong DNA adsorption and enhanced optical response are successfully achieved. The proposed biosensor yields sensitivity of 2235.4 nm/RIU when optically characterized by NaCl solution and sensitivity of 1.8295 nm/nM when tested with Leptospira cDNA. Both sensitivity values prove that the CQDs functionalized tapered optical fiber is capable of enhancing the sensor performance of standard tapered optical fiber sensor. Although the sensor LOD remained at 1.0 fM, higher sensor affinity towards Leptospira cDNA is achieved as the Kd value is lowered to 0.552 × 10−09 M compared to non-CQDs functionalized sensor. The sensor also exhibits high specificity when it exhibited minimal reaction to non-cDNA. High cross-reactivity is achieved as well when tested using Leptospira genomic DNA and C. Difficile DNA as a control. These findings have opened up a new way to develop more effective and direct detection tools for leptospirosis diagnosis.

Nurul Hida Zainuddin: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Hui Yee Chee: Resources, Supervision. Suraya Abdul Rashid: Resources. Muhammad Zamharir Ahmad: Resources. Muhammad Hafiz Abu Bakar: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Mohd Adzir Mahdi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Mohd Hanif Yaacob: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
